name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ لَيَقُولُونَ (٣٤)
```

```english
These ones say, (34)
```

```arabic
إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا مَوْتَتُنَا ٱلْأُولَىٰ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُنشَرِينَ (٣٥)
```

```english
‘It will be only our first death, and we shall not be resurrected. (35)
```

```arabic
فَأْتُوا۟ بِـَٔابَآئِنَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٣٦)
```

```english
Bring our fathers back [to life], if you are truthful.’ (36)
```

```arabic
أَهُمْ خَيْرٌ أَمْ قَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ (٣٧)
```

```english
Are they better, or the people of Tubba‘, and those who were before them? We destroyed them; indeed, they were guilty. (37)
```

```arabic
وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَـٰعِبِينَ (٣٨)
```

```english
We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. (38)
```

```arabic
مَا خَلَقْنَـٰهُمَآ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٩)
```

```english
We did not create them except with consummate wisdom; but most of them do not know. (39)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْفَصْلِ مِيقَـٰتُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٤٠)
```

```english
Indeed the Day of Judgement is the tryst for them all, (40)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ لَا يُغْنِى مَوْلًى عَن مَّوْلًى شَيْـًٔا وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٤١)
```

```english
the day when a friend will not avail a friend in any way, nor will they be helped, (41)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٤٢)
```

```english
except for him on whom Allah has mercy. Indeed He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (42)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ شَجَرَتَ ٱلزَّقُّومِ (٤٣)
```

```english
Indeed the tree of Zaqqum (43)
```

```arabic
طَعَامُ ٱلْأَثِيمِ (٤٤)
```

```english
will be the food of the sinful. (44)
```

```arabic
كَٱلْمُهْلِ يَغْلِى فِى ٱلْبُطُونِ (٤٥)
```

```english
Like molten copper it will boil in their bellies, (45)
```

```arabic
كَغَلْىِ ٱلْحَمِيمِ (٤٦)
```

```english
seething like boiling water. (46)
```

```arabic
خُذُوهُ فَٱعْتِلُوهُ إِلَىٰ سَوَآءِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٤٧)
```

```english
[The keepers of hell will be told,] ‘Seize him and drag him to the middle of hell, (47)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ صُبُّوا۟ فَوْقَ رَأْسِهِۦ مِنْ عَذَابِ ٱلْحَمِيمِ (٤٨)
```

```english
then pour over his head boiling water as punishment, (48)
```

```arabic
ذُقْ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْكَرِيمُ (٤٩)
```

```english
[and tell them,] “Taste this! Indeed you are the [self-styled] mighty and noble! (49)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا مَا كُنتُم بِهِۦ تَمْتَرُونَ (٥٠)
```

```english
This is what you used to doubt!’ (50)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى مَقَامٍ أَمِينٍ (٥١)
```

```english
Indeed the Godwary will be in a safe place, (51)
```

```arabic
فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (٥٢)
```

```english
amid gardens and springs, (52)
```

```arabic
يَلْبَسُونَ مِن سُندُسٍ وَإِسْتَبْرَقٍ مُّتَقَـٰبِلِينَ (٥٣)
```

```english
dressed in fine silk and brocade, sitting face to face. (53)
```

```arabic
كَذَٰلِكَ وَزَوَّجْنَـٰهُم بِحُورٍ عِينٍ (٥٤)
```

```english
So shall it be, and We shall wed them to black-eyed houris. (54)
```

```arabic
يَدْعُونَ فِيهَا بِكُلِّ فَـٰكِهَةٍ ءَامِنِينَ (٥٥)
```

```english
Secure [from any kind of harm,] there they will call for every kind of fruit [they wish]. (55)
```

```arabic
لَا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا ٱلْمَوْتَ إِلَّا ٱلْمَوْتَةَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ ۖ وَوَقَىٰهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٥٦)
```

```english
Other than the first death, they will not taste death therein, and He will save them from the punishment of hell (56)
```

```arabic
فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٥٧)
```

```english
—a grace from your Lord. That is the great success. (57)
```

```arabic
فَإِنَّمَا يَسَّرْنَـٰهُ بِلِسَانِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٥٨)
```

```english
Indeed We have made it simple in your language, so that they may take admonition. (58)
```

```arabic
فَٱرْتَقِبْ إِنَّهُم مُّرْتَقِبُونَ (٥٩)
```

```english
So wait! They [too] are waiting. (59)
```

## (Commentary)

After warning the people of worldly punishment and then of the punishment of the Hereafter, and illustrating the worldly punishment through what befell the people of Pharaoh—when Moses (عليه السلام) brought them the message from his Lord, but they denied him, so Allah seized them with the punishment of drowning and eradicated them—

The discourse returns to the punishment of the Hereafter. It mentions the people's denial of the Resurrection (المعاد) — *the return of the soul to the body for judgment* — and their claim that there is no life after the first death. He then argues for the proof of the Resurrection with logical demonstration (البرهان), followed by an announcement of some of what the criminals (المجرمون) will encounter of punishment in the Hereafter, and some of what the God-conscious (المتقون) — *those who guard themselves against evil through piety and awareness of Allah* — will encounter of everlasting bliss. At that point, the Surah concludes with what it began with: the revelation of the Book for the purpose of remembrance (التذكر), and the command to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to watch and wait.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ لَيَقُولُونَ إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا مَوْتَتُنَا الْأُولى وَما نَحْنُ بِمُنْشَرِينَ** "Indeed these say, 'It is nothing but our first death, and we shall not be resurrected.'" (44:34-35). This is a return to the beginning of the discourse from His saying: **بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ يَلْعَبُونَ** "Rather they are in doubt, playing around." (44:9). The demonstration "these" (هؤلاء) refers to the Quraysh and those associated with them among the pagan Arabs who denied the Resurrection. Their saying: "It is nothing but our first death" (إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا مَوْتَتُنَا الْأُولى) is intended to negate life after death, which necessitates the negation of the Resurrection, as evidenced by their subsequent saying: "and we shall not be resurrected" (وَما نَحْنُ بِمُنْشَرِينَ), meaning we shall not be raised. It is mentioned in *al-Kashshaf*: It is said, "Allah *anshara* (أنشر) the dead" or "*nasharahum* (نشرهم)" when He raises them. End quote.

Thus, their saying: "It is nothing but our first death" (إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا مَوْتَتُنَا الْأُولى)—the pronoun "it" (هي) refers to the ultimate end (العاقبة) and the finality (النهاية). That is: the end of our affair and the finality of our existence and life is nothing but our first death; we perish by it and there is no life after it ever.

As for the reason for qualifying the death in the verse as "the first" (الأولى), it has been said that it is not a restrictive qualification (قيد احترازي), as there is no necessary correlation between the first and the last, or between the first and the second. It is possible for something to be "first" without there being a "second" for it, nor another in opposition to it.

There is another view mentioned by al-Zamakhshari in *al-Kashshaf*, where he said: "If you ask: The discourse was regarding the second life, not death, so why was it not said: 'It is nothing but our first life, and we shall not be resurrected,' as it was said: 'It is nothing but our life in this world, and we shall not be raised'? And what is the meaning of His saying: 'except our first death' (إِلَّا مَوْتَتُنَا الْأُولى)? And what is the meaning of mentioning 'the first'? It is as if they were promised another death such that they negated and denied it while affirming the first."

"I say: Its meaning—and Allah is the Granter of success to what is right—is that they were told: 'Indeed you shall die a death followed by life, just as a death preceded you which was followed by life,' and that is the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: **وَكُنْتُمْ أَمْواتاً فَأَحْياكُمْ ثُمَّ يُمِيتُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُحْيِيكُمْ** '...and you were lifeless and He gave you life, then He will cause you to die, then He will bring you to life...' (2:28). So they said: 'It is nothing but our first death,' meaning that the death which is characterized by being followed by life is nothing but the first death, not the second death. This attribute you describe death with—of life following it—belongs only to the first death specifically. Thus, there is no difference in meaning between this and His saying: 'It is nothing but our life in this world.'" End quote.

It is possible to explain it in a third way: that they said: "It is nothing but our first death" after hearing His saying, the Exalted: **قالُوا رَبَّنا أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ** "They will say, 'Our Lord! Twice did You cause us to die, and twice did You give us life...'" (40:11). It has already been mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of that verse that the first causing of death is the death after the life of this world, and the second causing of death is that which occurs after the life of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection*. Thus, in their saying: "It is nothing but our first death," they are negating the second death which is inseparable from the life of the intermediate realm, which is life after death; for they view the death of a human being as his annihilation (انعدام) and the nullification (بطلان) of his essence (الذات).

It is possible to explain it in a fourth way: that the qualification of "the first" (الأولى) refers to the narration (الحكاية) rather than the narrated content (المحكي). That is, what they actually said was: "It is nothing but our death," and the meaning of the discourse is that these people negate life after death and say: "It is nothing but our death," meaning the first death of the two deaths we mentioned in our saying: "They will say, 'Our Lord! Twice did You cause us to die...'"

The four views differ in their proximity to understanding; the closest is the third, then the fourth, then the first.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَأْتُوا بِآبائِنا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** "Then bring back our fathers, if you are truthful." (44:36). This is the completion of the people's speech and a challenge from them to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the believers in him, as they used to mention the Resurrection (البعث) and the restoration of life (الإحياء) to them. They argued to reject the restoration of life after death by saying: "Then bring back our fathers, if you are truthful," meaning: let our past forefathers be brought back to life through your prayer or whatever means you have taken, so that we may know your truthfulness in your claim that the dead will be brought to life and that death is not annihilation.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَهُمْ خَيْرٌ أَمْ قَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ وَالَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ أَهْلَكْناهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا مُجْرِمِينَ** "Are they better, or the people of Tubba‘ and those who were before them? We destroyed them; indeed they were criminals." (44:37). This is a threat to the people of destruction (الإهلاك), just as the people of Tubba‘ and the nations before them were destroyed.

This **Tubba‘** (تبع) was one of the kings of the Himyarites in Yemen, and his name, according to what has been mentioned, was As‘ad Abu Karib; it is also said: Sa‘d Abu Karib. A portion of his story will follow in the discussion of narrations (البحث الروائي). In the discourse, there is a subtle indication of the safety of Tubba‘ himself from destruction.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ ما خَلَقْناهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with consummate wisdom, but most of them do not know." (44:38-39). The dual pronoun in His saying: "and whatever is between them" (وَما بَيْنَهُما) refers to the two genera of the heavens and the earth, which is why it was not pluralized. The preposition *bi* (بـ) in His saying "with truth" (بِالْحَقِّ) denotes accompaniment (الملابسة), meaning: We did not create them except as being clothed in truth. Some have allowed it to denote causality (السببية), meaning: We did not create them for any reason except for the sake of the Truth, which is faith, obedience, resurrection, and recompense; however, its remoteness is not hidden.

The content of the two verses is a demonstrative proof (حجة برهانية) for the establishment of the Resurrection. Its formulation is that if there were not beyond this world a stable and lasting world, but rather Allah continued to bring things into existence and then annihilate them, then bring other things into existence and annihilate them, giving life to this one and then causing him to die, and giving life to another, and so on—He would be a player (لاعب) in His action, acting in vain (عابث). Play is impossible for Him, the Exalted; rather, His action is truth (حق) and has a sound purpose. Therefore, there is another world, lasting and eternal, to which things transition. What is in this worldly, vanishing, and perishing realm is a prelude to the transition to that world, which is the life of the Hereafter.

We have detailed the discussion on this proof in the commentary of verse 16 of Surah al-Anbiya and verse 27 of Surah Sad, so let it be referred to.

His saying: "but most of them do not know" (وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ) is a reproach to them for their ignorance.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ يَوْمَ الْفَصْلِ مِيقاتُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** "Indeed the Day of Judgment is the tryst for them all" (44:40). This is an explanation of the attribute of the Day established by the previous proof, which is the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) wherein mankind will stand before the Lord of all the worlds.

Allah named it the Day of Sorting (يوم الفصل) — *the decisive day when truth is distinguished from falsehood* — because on it, a separation is made between truth and falsehood, between the one on the right and the one on the wrong, and between the God-conscious and the criminals; or because it is the Day of the Decisive Judgment (القضاء الفصل) from Him, the Exalted.

His saying: "the tryst for them all" (مِيقاتُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ) means the appointed time for all people, or the appointed time for those previously mentioned: the people of Tubba‘, the people of Pharaoh, those before them, the Quraysh, and others.

His saying, the Exalted: **يَوْمَ لا يُغْنِي مَوْلًى عَنْ مَوْلًى شَيْئاً وَلا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ** "the day when a friend will not avail a friend in any way, nor will they be helped" (44:41). This is an explanation of the Day of Sorting. The *mawla* (المولى) is the companion who has the right to manage the affairs of his companion; it is applied both to the one who takes charge of an affair and the one whose affair is taken charge of. The first *mawla* in the verse is the former, and the second is the latter.

The verse first negates that a friend will avail his friend on that day, and secondly informs that they will not be helped. The difference between the two meanings is that "availing" (الإغناء) occurs when the one providing aid acts independently, and the one being aided has no part in it; whereas "help" (النصرة) occurs when the one being helped possesses some incomplete means of victory, and it is completed for him by the help of the helper.

The reason for the negation of availing and help on that day is that the causes (الأسباب) that are effective in the life of this world fall away on the Day of Resurrection. He, the Exalted, said: **وَتَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ الْأَسْبابُ** "...and all their means are cut off" (2:166), and He said: **فَزَيَّلْنا بَيْنَهُمْ** "...then We shall set them apart from one another" (10:28).

His saying, the Exalted: **إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ اللهُ إِنَّهُ هُوة الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ** "except him on whom Allah has mercy. Indeed He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful." (44:42). This is an exception from the pronoun in "nor will they be helped" (وَلا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ). The verse is among the proofs for intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — on that day. The detailed discussion on intercession has already been presented in the first volume of this book.

This is based on the assumption that the pronoun in "nor will they be helped" refers to all people, as is apparent.

However, if it refers to the disbelievers (الكفار), as has been said, then the exception is disconnected (منقطع), and the meaning is: But those whom Allah has mercied—and they are the God-conscious—they are in no need of a friend to avail them or a helper to help them.

As for what some have allowed—that it is a connected exception (استثناء متصل) from "friend" (مَوْلًى)—its invalidity has become clear from what we have previously stated. For availing only occurs when a person possesses none of the means of salvation, and whoever is in this state, no one can avail him, nor is there any exception. Intercession is a form of help that requires some means of salvation, which is the pleasing religion (الدين المرضي), as previously mentioned in the discussion on intercession. Yes, it can be explained by what will come in the narration of al-Shahham.

His saying: "Indeed He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful" (إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ) means the Overcomer (الغالب) whom nothing can overcome so as to prevent Him from punishing whom He wills to punish, and the Bestower of good upon whom He wills to have mercy. The suitability of these two Noble Names to the contents of the verses is manifest.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ شَجَرَةَ الزَّقُّومِ طَعامُ الْأَثِيمِ** "Indeed the tree of Zaqqum is the food of the sinful." (44:43-44). The discussion on the tree of Zaqqum has already been presented in the commentary of Surah al-Saffat. The **sinful** (الأثيم) is the one in whom sin has become established, either by persistence in disobedience or by the multiplicity of sins. This verse, until the completion of eight verses, is an explanation of the state of the people of the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: **كَالْمُهْلِ يَغْلِي فِي الْبُطُونِ كَغَلْيِ الْحَمِيمِ** "Like molten copper, it will boil in their bellies, like the boiling of boiling water." (44:45-46). **Molten metal** (المهل) is melted copper, lead, or similar substances. Boiling (الغلي) and effervescence (الغليان) are well-known. **Scalding water** (الحميم) is water that is intensely hot. His saying: "Like molten metal" is a second predicate for "Indeed" (إِنَّ), just as His saying: "food of the sinful" is the first predicate. His saying: "it will boil in their bellies like the boiling of scalding water" is a third predicate. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **خُذُوهُ فَاعْتِلُوهُ إِلى سَواءِ الْجَحِيمِ** "[It will be said,] ‘Seize him and drag him into the midst of hell." (44:47). **Dragging** (الاعتلاء) is to shake and push with violence. The "midst of hell" (سواء الجحيم) is its center. The address is to the angels entrusted with the Fire, meaning: We say to the angels, "Take the sinful one and push him violently into the center of the Fire so that it surrounds him." He, the Exalted, said: **وَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ** "...and indeed hell besieges the faithless" (9:49).

His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ صُبُّوا فَوْقَ رَأْسِهِ مِنْ عَذابِ الْحَمِيمِ** "Then pour over his head the punishment of boiling water." (44:48). It seems that "punishment" (عذاب) refers to that by which one is punished, and its annexation to "scalding water" (الحميم) is explanatory (بيانية). The meaning is: Then pour over his head some of the scalding water by which he is punished.

His saying, the Exalted: **ذُقْ إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْكَرِيمُ** "‘Taste [this]! Indeed you are the [self-styled] mighty and generous!" (44:49). This is an address directed to the sinful one while he is enduring punishment after punishment. Describing him with might (العزة) and nobility (الكرامة) despite his state of humiliation and baseness is a form of mockery (استهزاء) to intensify his punishment. He used to see for himself in the world a might and nobility that would never leave him, as appears from what Allah, the Glorified, narrated of his saying: **وَما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً وَلَئِنْ رُجِعْتُ إِلى رَبِّي إِنَّ لِي عِنْدَهُ لَلْحُسْنى** "...I do not think the Hour will ever set in, and even if I am returned to my Lord, I will indeed have the best with Him." (41:50).

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ هذا ما كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تَمْتَرُونَ** "This is indeed what you used to doubt.’" (44:50). **Doubting** (الامتراء) is skepticism and suspicion. The verse is the completion of their saying to him: "Taste..." etc. In it is an emphasis and an informing of them of their error and slip in the world when they doubted what they today witness of the punishment with ocular vision. For this reason, the enduring of punishment was expressed as "tasting" (الذوق), because the complete perception of the pain of painful things and the pleasure of pleasurable things is expressed as tasting.

It is possible that the verse is a new sentence (استئناف) from the speech of Allah, the Glorified, addressing the disbelievers after mentioning their state on the Day of Resurrection. This might be supported by His saying: "you [plural] used to doubt" (كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تَمْتَرُونَ) with a plural address, whereas the address in the previous verses was singular.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي مَقامٍ أَمِينٍ** "Indeed the God-conscious will be in a safe station" (44:51). **Station** (المقام) is the place of standing in the sense of stability and rootedness; hence it is also interpreted as the place of residence. **Safe** (الأمين) is an attribute from security (الأمن) in the sense of not being afflicted by anything disliked. The meaning is: The God-conscious—on the Day of Resurrection—are stable in a place characterized by security from being afflicted by anything disliked at all.

From this, it becomes clear that attributing security to the "station" by describing the station as "safe" is a metaphorical attribution (مجاز في النسبة).

His saying, the Exalted: **فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ** "amid gardens and springs" (44:52). This is an explanation of His saying: "in a safe station." The springs were made a vessel (ظرف) for them by virtue of proximity and their existence in the gardens which are the vessel. The gardens were pluralized in consideration of the diversity of their types, or because each of them alone has a garden or more.

His saying, the Exalted: **يَلْبَسُونَ مِنْ سُندُسٍ وَإِسْتَبْرَقٍ مُتَقابِلِينَ** "clad in silk and brocade, facing one another." (44:53). **Silk** (السندس) is fine silk, and **brocade** (الإستبرق) is thick silk; both are Arabized from Persian.

His saying: "facing one another" (مُتَقابِلِينَ) means they face each other for sociability, as there is no evil or disliked thing among them because they are in a safe station.

His saying, the Exalted: **كَذلِكَ وَزَوَّجْناهُمْ بِحُورٍ عِينٍ** "So shall it be, and We shall wed them to black-eyed houris." (44:54). Meaning: the matter is as such, i.e., as We have described. What is meant by "wedding them to houris" is making them companions to them, from *zawj* (الزوج) meaning companion (القرين), which is the linguistic root of marriage. **Houris** (الحور) is the plural of *hawra’* (حوراء), meaning one with intense blackness and whiteness of the eye, or one with black pupils like gazelles. **Large-eyed** (العين) is the plural of *‘ayna’* (عيناء), meaning one with large eyes. The apparent meaning of His speech, the Exalted, is that the *hur al-‘ayn* are other than the women of the world who enter Paradise.

His saying, the Exalted: **يَدْعُونَ فِيها بِكُلِّ فاكِهَةٍ آمِنِينَ** "There they will call for every kind of fruit, in security." (44:55). Meaning: secure from its harm.

His saying, the Exalted: **لا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا الْمَوْتَ إِلَّا الْمَوْتَةَ الْأُولى وَوَقاهُمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ** "They will not taste death therein, except the first death, and He will spare them the punishment of hell" (44:56). Meaning: they are in the Garden of Eternity, alive with an eternal life that death does not touch.

A problem has been raised regarding the verse: that the exception of the first death from His saying: "They will not taste death therein" implies that they taste the first death *therein*, whereas the opposite is certainly intended. In another formulation: the first death is the death of the world, and it has already passed for the people of Paradise. To be characterized in the future by a past matter is certainly impossible; so what is the meaning of excepting the first death from the absence of tasting in the future?

There is another problem they have not addressed: it has already been mentioned in His saying, the Exalted: **رَبَّنا أَمَتَّنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ** "Our Lord! Twice did You cause us to die, and twice did You give us life" (40:11), that between the life of this world and the Hour there are two deaths: a death by transition from the world to the intermediate realm, and a death by transition from the intermediate realm to the Hereafter. It is apparent that what is meant by "the first death" in the verse is the death of the world that transfers man to the intermediate realm. Suppose we resolved the exception of the first death in some way; why then was the second death not excepted? What is the difference between them when they are two deaths they tasted before entering the Garden of Eternity?

The answer to the first problem is that the exception is disconnected (منقطع), and the meaning is: But they have already tasted the first death in the world and it has passed, so the generality of His saying: "They will not taste death therein" remains as it is.

On the assumption that the exception is not disconnected, "except" (إلا) means "other than" (سوى), and "except the first death" (إِلَّا الْمَوْتَةَ الْأُولى) is a substitute (بدل) for "death" (الْمَوْتَ) and is not part of the exception at all. The meaning is: They will not taste therein any death other than the first death. As for the first death, they have already tasted it, and it is impossible for it to return and be tasted, as it is the first.

Other answers have been given that are not worth considering. You are aware that neither of the two views explains the characterization of the death as "the first," and several views on this have already been presented in the commentary of His saying: "It is nothing but our first death."

As for the second problem, it can be answered by the second answer mentioned above, since there are two deaths: the first death, which transfers man from the world to the intermediate realm, and the second death, which transfers him from the intermediate realm to the Hereafter. Thus, if "except" (إِلَّا) in His saying: "except the first death" means "other than," and the whole phrase is a substitute for "death," the verse is intended to negate anything other than the first death, which is the second death—the death of the intermediate realm. Thus, there is no death in the Garden of the Hereafter: not the death of the world, because it was realized for them previously, nor anything other than the death of the world, which is the death of the intermediate realm. By this, the reason for qualifying the death as "the first" becomes clear.

His saying: "and He will spare them the punishment of hell" (وَوَقاهُمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ). **Protection** (الوقاية) is to preserve a thing from what hurts and harms it. The meaning is: He preserved them from the punishment of hell. Mentioning their protection from the punishment of hell along with the negation of death from them is a completion of the division of disliked things. That is: they are protected from transitioning from one abode to another and from the state of Paradise to another state—which is death—and they are protected from transitioning from a happy state to a wretched state—which is the punishment of hell.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَضْلاً مِنْ رَبِّكَ ذلِكَ هُوة الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ** "as a bounty from your Lord. That is the great success." (44:57). This is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for what was previously mentioned of honor and blessing. It could also be an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) or an object of purpose (مفعول له). In any case, it is a favor (تفضل) from Him, the Exalted, without any entitlement (استحقاق) from the servants—an entitlement that would necessitate or obligate Him to reward. For He, the Exalted, is the Owner (مالك) who is not owned; nothing can dictate to Him. It is only His promise to His servants, and then He informed that He does not break His promise. The detailed discussion of this meaning has already been presented in previous sections.

His saying: "That is the great success" (ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ). **Success** (الفوز) is the attainment of what is desired, and its being a "great success" is because it is the ultimate happiness a human being can achieve.

His saying, the Exalted: **فإِنَّما يَسَّرْناهُ بِلِسانِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ** "Indeed We have made it simple in your language so that they may take admonition." (44:58). This is a derivation (تفريع) from all that has preceded from the beginning of the Surah until here, and a summary (فذلكة) of the whole. **Simplification** (التيسير) is making easy. The pronoun refers to the Book, and what is meant by the Prophet's language (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is Arabic.

The meaning is: We have only made the Qur'an easy—meaning the understanding of its objectives—through the Arabic language so that they—meaning your people—might take admonition. Thus, the verse is close in meaning to His saying: **إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ** "Indeed We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason." (43:3).

It has been said: The meaning of simplifying the Book in the Prophet's language (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is making it flow upon his tongue while he was unlettered (أمي), neither reading nor writing, so that it might be a sign (آية) of the truthfulness of his prophethood. This is remote from the context of the summary.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَارْتَقِبْ إِنَّهُمْ مُرْتَقِبُونَ** "So watch! They [too] are watching." (44:59). It is as if this is derived from what is derived from the previous verse. The sum of the meaning is: We simplified it in Arabic in the hope that they would take admonition, but they did not; rather, they are in doubt, playing around, and waiting for the punishment that cannot be averted from the deniers. So wait for the punishment; indeed, they are waiting for it.

Thus, applying the term "watchers" (المرتقبين) to the people is by way of sarcasm (تهكم). Among the weak opinions is the saying of those who claim that in the verse is a command to leave them alone, and that it was abrogated (منسوخة) by the verse of the sword.

## (Discussion of Narrations)

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَهُمْ خَيْرٌ أَمْ قَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ** "Are they better, or the people of Tubba‘..." (44:37), Sahl ibn Sa‘id narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he said: **لا تسبوا تبعا فإنه كان قد أسلم** — "Do not revile Tubba‘, for he had indeed embraced Islam."

I say: This meaning was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn ‘Abbas, and also from Ibn ‘Asakir from ‘Ata’ ibn Abi Rabah from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In the same source, al-Walid ibn Sabih narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) that he said: "Indeed Tubba‘ said to the Aws and the Khazraj: 'Stay here until this Prophet comes forth; as for me, if I were to reach him, I would serve him and go forth with him.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abu Nu‘aym narrated in *al-Dala’il* from ‘Abdullah ibn Salam who said: "Tubba‘ did not die until he believed in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—when the Jews of Yathrib used to inform him."

I say: The reports regarding the matter of Tubba‘ are many, and in some of them, it is mentioned that he was the first to clothe the Kaaba.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration to Zayd al-Shahham, who said: "Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) said to me while we were on the road on a Friday night: 'Recite some Qur'an, for it is Friday night.' So I recited: **إِنَّ يَوْمَ الْفَصْلِ مِيقاتُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ**—**يَوْمَ لا يُغْنِي مَوْلًى عَنْ مَوْلًى شَيْئاً**—**وَلا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ اللهُ** 'Indeed the Day of Judgment is the tryst for them all—the day when a friend will not avail a friend in any way, nor will they be helped, except him on whom Allah has mercy.' Then Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) said: 'By Allah, we are the ones whom Allah excepted—so we used to avail them.'"

I say: He (عليه السلام) is referring to intercession (الشفاعة), and he took the exception as referring to the first "friend" (مولى).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: Then He said: **إِنَّ شَجَرَةَ الزَّقُّومِ طَعامُ الْأَثِيمِ** "Indeed the tree of Zaqqum is the food of the sinful." (44:43-44). It was revealed concerning Abu Jahl ibn Hisham. Regarding His saying: **كَالْمُهْلِ** "Like molten metal," he said: "Molten metal is melted brass." **يَغْلِي فِي الْبُطُونِ كَغَلْيِ الْحَمِيمِ** "it will boil in their bellies like the boiling of boiling water"—and it is that which has been heated and reached the ultimate limit.

I say: From the paths of the Ahl al-Sunnah as well, there are narrations that support the revelation of the verse concerning Abu Jahl.
